g.data-narration: Welcome to Computer Networks! This module covers fundamentals essential for every CSE engineer.
A computer network is a collection of interconnected computing devices that communicate and share resources through transmission media and protocols.
g.text: A collection of interconnected computing devices that can
communicate and share resources with each other.

g.data-narration: Computer networks enable email, video streaming, cloud services, and online collaboration - the backbone of modern digital communication.
g.text: • Enable seamless data exchange between devices globally
• Power modern communication: email, video calls, streaming

g.data-narration: A network consists of nodes, NICs for connectivity, transmission media, network devices like routers and switches, and communication protocols.
Networks are classified by coverage area: PAN, LAN, MAN, WAN, and CAN.
LAN covers a building with high speed up to 100 Gbps, while WAN covers large areas with lower speed and is owned by service providers.
Metropolitan Area Networks cover cities or large campuses, connecting multiple LANs with moderate to high speeds.
g.text: • Connects multiple LANs | Used by cable TV operators

g.data-narration: Network topology defines the arrangement of nodes and connections, affecting performance, reliability, and cost.
g.text: The arrangement of nodes and connections in a network.
Types: Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree | Affects: Performance | Reliability | Cost

g.data-narration: In bus topology, all nodes connect to a single backbone cable. Failure of the backbone breaks entire network.
g.text: ✓ Simple, low cost | ✗ Single point of failure

g.data-narration: Star topology connects all nodes to a central hub or switch. Most common in modern networks.
g.text: ✓ Easy management, fault isolation | ✗ Central device failure

g.data-narration: Ring topology connects nodes in a closed loop with data traveling one direction. Uses token passing.
g.text: ✓ Equal access | ✗ One node failure breaks ring

g.data-narration: Mesh topology connects each node to multiple others, providing high redundancy. Full mesh has n(n-1)/2 connections.
Tree topology combines bus and star topologies in a hierarchical structure, commonly used in large organizations.
g.text: ✓ Scalable, hierarchical | ✗ Root failure impacts all

g.data-narration: Transmission modes: simplex (one-way), half-duplex (two-way not simultaneously), full-duplex (simultaneous two-way).
OSI model has 7 layers: Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, and Application.
TCP/IP model has 4 layers: Network Interface, Internet, Transport, and Application. It's the practical implementation used on the Internet.
OSI has 7 layers while TCP/IP has 4. Application layer maps to OSI's top 3, Network Access to bottom 2.
g.text: TCP/IP App → OSI: App + Pres + Sess
TCP/IP Net → OSI: Net + Data Link + Phys

g.data-narration: This module covered network fundamentals: components, classifications, topologies, transmission modes, and the OSI and TCP/IP models.
g.text: • Networks connect devices for resource sharing and communication
• LAN/MAN/WAN classification by geographic area | Topologies: Bus, Star, Ring, Mesh, Tree
• OSI 7-layer model vs TCP/IP 4-layer model | Transmission: Simplex, Half-Duplex, Full-Duplex
